verses.commentary: What advantage is there in being a Jew, or what value is there in circumcision? If inward reality matters more than outward signs (2:28-29), does Jewish identity have any value? Paul anticipates the objection and answers it directly.

Much in every way! First of all, the Jews have been entrusted with the very words of God. The oracles of God (ta logia tou theou, τὰ λόγια τοῦ θεοῦ) are the Scriptures. Israel's privilege was receiving divine revelation. This is an immense advantage, though it does not guarantee salvation.

What if some were unfaithful? Will their unfaithfulness nullify God's faithfulness? Jewish unbelief does not cancel God's promises. Human failure does not undermine divine faithfulness. God's character is not contingent on human response.

Not at all! Let God be true, and every human being a liar (Psalm 116:11). God's truthfulness stands regardless of human falsehood. God will be proved right when He speaks and will prevail when He judges (Psalm 51:4). God's integrity is absolute and unassailable.

But if our unrighteousness brings out God's righteousness more clearly, what shall we say? That God is unjust in bringing His wrath on us? Paul uses a human argument (addressing a potential objection). If sin magnifies God's righteousness, is it unjust for God to judge us? This is a false conclusion.
Certainly not! If that were so, how could God judge the world? God's role as judge presupposes His justice. If God were unjust, He could not judge. Universal judgment requires a perfectly just Judge.

If my falsehood enhances God's truthfulness and increases His glory, why am I still condemned as a sinner? This is the objection restated. The logic is flawed: sin is never justified by its unintended consequences. God's glory does not excuse human sin.

Why not say, 'Let us do evil that good may result'? Some slanderously claimed Paul taught this. Their condemnation is just. This is a complete distortion of grace. Licentiousness is not the fruit of the gospel; holiness is.

What shall we conclude then? Do we have any advantage? Not at all! Jews and Gentiles alike are all under the power of sin. Universal guilt levels the ground. No ethnic or religious group has moral superiority. All are enslaved to sin apart from grace.

As it is written: There is no one righteous, not even one (Psalm 14:1-3, 53:1-3). Paul begins a catena (chain) of Old Testament quotations proving universal sinfulness. Total depravity: no one meets God's standard of righteousness.

There is no one who understands; there is no one who seeks God. Sin corrupts both intellect and will. Humans do not naturally seek God; they flee from Him (Genesis 3:8). Apart from grace, no one pursues God.

All have turned away; they have together become worthless. There is no one who does good, not even one. Universal rebellion. Humanity is collectively corrupt. Worthless (achreioi, ἄχρειοι) means unprofitable, useless for God's purposes. Not even one excludes all exceptions.

Their throats are open graves; their tongues practice deceit (Psalm 5:9). The poison of vipers is on their lips (Psalm 140:3). Speech reveals the heart's corruption. Deadly, deceitful, poisonous words flow from sinful hearts.

Their mouths are full of cursing and bitterness (Psalm 10:7). Speech is a window into the soul. What fills the mouth reveals what fills the heart. Cursing and bitterness are symptoms of inner corruption.
Their feet are swift to shed blood (Proverbs 1:16, Isaiah 59:7). Violence comes easily to sinful humanity. The readiness to harm others reflects the depth of human depravity.
Ruin and misery mark their ways. Sin leaves a trail of destruction. The path of the wicked produces brokenness and suffering, both for themselves and others.
The way of peace they do not know (Isaiah 59:8). Sin disrupts shalom. Apart from God, there is no true peace. Humanity apart from God is characterized by conflict, not peace.

There is no fear of God before their eyes (Psalm 36:1). The root problem: lack of reverence for God. When God is not feared, all restraint is removed. The fear of the Lord is the beginning of wisdom (Proverbs 9:10); without it, folly reigns.

Whatever the law says, it says to those who are under the law, so that every mouth may be silenced and the whole world held accountable to God. The law's purpose is to stop all excuses. It reveals sin and silences self-justification. Universal accountability before God is the result.

No one will be declared righteous in God's sight by the works of the law; rather, through the law we become conscious of our sin. The law cannot justify; it can only expose sin. Works of the law cannot save because they only reveal our failure. The law's role is diagnostic, not curative.

But now apart from the law the righteousness of God has been made known, to which the Law and the Prophets testify. A monumental shift. Righteousness apart from law-keeping is revealed. The Old Testament pointed forward to this (Genesis 15:6, Habakkuk 2:4). This is not a new gospel but the fulfillment of the old covenant.

This righteousness is given through faith in Jesus Christ to all who believe. There is no difference between Jew and Gentile. Righteousness comes through faith, not works. The ground is level at the cross. Ethnic distinctions are irrelevant in salvation.

For all have sinned and fall short of the glory of God. Universal sinfulness (past tense: have sinned) and present condition (fall short, present tense). The glory of God is the standard; all miss it. Sin is not just breaking rules but failing to reflect God's glory.

And all are justified freely by His grace through the redemption that came by Christ Jesus. Justification is by grace (unearned favor), not merit. Freely (dōrean, δωρεάν) means as a gift. Redemption (apolytrōsis, ἀπολύτρωσις) implies a ransom paid. Christ's work is the basis of our justification.

God presented Christ as a sacrifice of atonement (hilastērion, ἱλαστήριον, propitiation or mercy seat), through the shedding of His blood, to be received by faith. Christ's blood satisfies God's wrath. This demonstrates God's righteousness, because in His forbearance He had left sins committed beforehand unpunished. God's past forbearance might seem unjust; the cross vindicates God's justice while providing mercy.

He did it to demonstrate His righteousness at the present time, so as to be just and the one who justifies those who have faith in Jesus. The cross solves the dilemma: How can God be just and justify sinners? The cross upholds justice (sin is punished) and provides justification (Christ bears the penalty). God is both just and justifier.

Where, then, is boasting? It is excluded. Because of what law? The law that requires works? No, because of the law that requires faith. Boasting is eliminated because salvation is by faith, not works. Faith receives; works earn. Receiving a gift leaves no room for boasting.

For we maintain that a person is justified by faith apart from the works of the law. This is the thesis of Romans. Justification is sola fide (by faith alone). Works of the law do not contribute to justification. Faith alone unites us to Christ, whose righteousness is credited to us.

Or is God the God of Jews only? Is He not the God of Gentiles too? Yes, of Gentiles too. God is universal, not tribal. Salvation by works would favor Jews who have the law. Salvation by faith is available to all.

Since there is only one God, who will justify the circumcised by faith and the uncircumcised through that same faith. One God, one way of salvation. Jew and Gentile are justified the same way: by faith. The prepositions (by, through) are synonymous here, emphasizing the singular means of justification.

Do we, then, nullify the law by this faith? Not at all! Rather, we uphold the law. Faith does not abolish the law. It fulfills the law's purpose: pointing to Christ. The law condemns; Christ saves. The law reveals the need for grace. Faith upholds the law by acknowledging its verdict and trusting Christ who fulfilled it.
